name: Zora Neale Hurston
practice: Folklore collection, Black cultural documentation, participant observation as native daughter
approach: She studied her own community — not as outsider anthropologist but as native daughter returning with ethnographic tools. Mules and Men documented hoodoo, work songs, folktales — the living culture that academic anthropology couldn't see because it only looked from outside.
body_of_work: Mules and Men (1935) — first collection of African American folklore by a Black woman. Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937). Tell My Horse (1938) — Haitian vodou. Barnard-trained anthropologist who brought insider knowledge to the discipline.
why_instructive: She proves that the best community data comes from the community itself. The dome's community layer should be informed by people who live inside it, not consultants who visit.

dome_relevance.8: Community — insider documentation of Black community culture
dome_relevance.11: Creativity — folklore as living creative practice
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing — joy, humor, and beauty within oppression

sphere_relevance.5: Social — deep understanding of community from inside
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — oral tradition carrying history across generations
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — cultural performance as community activation
